transcript.text: Today we're going to talk about embeddings, which are fundamental to modern natural language processing and retrieval systems.
An embedding is a way to represent text as a vector of numbers. Each word, sentence, or document gets mapped to a point in a high-dimensional space.
The key property of embeddings is that semantically similar texts end up close to each other in this vector space.
For example, the embeddings for 'cat' and 'kitten' would be very close, while 'cat' and 'car' would be far apart.
Modern embedding models are typically based on transformer neural networks, which were introduced in the famous 'Attention is All You Need' paper.
Popular embedding models include BERT, Sentence-BERT, and more recently, models like all-MiniLM which are optimized for speed and efficiency.
To measure similarity between embeddings, we typically use cosine similarity, which calculates the angle between two vectors.
A cosine similarity of 1 means the vectors are identical, 0 means they're orthogonal, and -1 means they point in opposite directions.
In RAG systems, we use embeddings to convert both our document chunks and user queries into vectors, allowing us to find relevant information through semantic search.
One advantage of embedding-based search over traditional keyword search is that it can find relevant documents even when they don't contain the exact query terms.
For instance, searching for 'president of USA' would correctly retrieve documents about the 'American head of state' even though the words don't match exactly.
When building a RAG system, choosing the right embedding model is important. You need to balance between model size, speed, and quality.
Thanks for watching! Next time we'll explore vector databases and how to efficiently store and search millions of embeddings.
